title: Setting and Jumping to Marks
subtitle: {key:m}{a-z} sets a mark. {key:'}{a-z} jumps to its line. {key:`}{a-z} jumps to its exact spot.
summary: Marks are named bookmarks. Lowercase marks are buffer-local; uppercase marks are global across files.

blocks.text: Marks are persistent bookmarks. {key:m}{key:a} sets mark `a` at the cursor. {key:'}{key:a} or {key:`}{key:a} jumps back to it. The whole alphabet is at your disposal — 26 lowercase marks per buffer plus 26 uppercase that work across files.

blocks.label: Jump to line of mark a
Jump to exact position of mark a
Set global mark A (works across files)

blocks.title: Marks Are a Per-Buffer Dictionary
blocks.text:
Each buffer has a `mark → (line, col)` dictionary. `{a–z}` are local to that buffer. `{A–Z}` are stored in *Vim's* shared mark table along with the file path — that's why uppercase marks can jump you to *another file*.

Global marks survive across sessions if `shada` (Neovim) / `viminfo` (Vim) is enabled. So {key:m}{key:Q} on a file you reread weekly takes you straight back, even after a restart.

Marks update automatically when you edit *above* them — Vim shifts their line number to track the same logical position. They don't update when you delete the line they're on; the mark just disappears.

`:marks` shows the full list.

{key:'} and {key:`} are also *jumps* — they push the previous position to the jumplist. {key:Ctrl-O} returns. So you can mark a spot, wander, jump back, then {key:Ctrl-O} to where you were before the jump.
{key:d}{key:'}{key:a} deletes from the cursor to the line of mark `a`, linewise. {key:d}{key:`}{key:a} deletes to the exact position. They're motions like everything else in Vim.

rows: Jump to exact position of mark
